text.title: Onboarding Patterns, Segmentation, and Optimization
text.summary: Five onboarding patterns matched to product types, per-segment routing, critical-path definition, and step-reduction tactics.

rule.statement: Select one onboarding pattern per user segment based on product surface and user intent; never apply the same pattern to all segments.
rule.rationale: Generic patterns for all segments compound drop-off — individual users get team setup screens, evaluators get power-user prompts.

list.item: Template-first: start with pre-built example, let users customize. Best for configurable workflow tools (Notion, Trello, Canva). Fastest path to Aha for new users with no prior context.
Wizard onboarding: step-by-step guided setup with 2-4 personalization questions. Best for products requiring integration/configuration (Slack, Asana, Zapier).
Interactive tutorial: learn by doing inside a sandboxed demo environment. Best for spatial UIs or complex interactions (Figma, Duolingo). Requires engineering investment.
Self-serve exploration: minimal guidance with optional help. Best for simple, intuitive products (Instagram, Pinterest). Avoid for products with configurable state.
Concierge onboarding: high-touch 1:1 setup call. Best for enterprise B2B or products with high switching cost. Does not scale; gates growth on CS headcount.

rule.statement: Ask exactly one segmentation question at signup to route users to per-segment onboarding flows. Choices should map directly to product use cases, not demographic personas.
rule.rationale: A single routing question + segment-specific flow outperforms perfectly generic flow. More than one routing question increases drop-off.

example.note: One question, three routes mapping to different onboarding patterns.
example.code: What brings you here? [ ] Personal use → Quick-start template flow [ ] Team collaboration → Team setup wizard [ ] Just exploring → Interactive demo

rule.statement: Define the critical path as the minimum steps required to reach the activation event — typically 3-5 steps. Everything else is optional and triggered contextually after activation.
rule.rationale: Every additional required step loses users. Moving optional steps (profile photo, preferences, integrations) to contextual triggers preserves path completion without losing feature depth.

example.note: Activation event: "created 1 project with 3+ tasks". Critical path has 3 required steps.
example.code:
REQUIRED (critical path): 1. Email signup 2. Create first project (with sample task pre-loaded) 3. Add 2 more tasks → activates
OPTIONAL (contextual triggers post-activation): - Profile photo (trigger: first share action) - Timezone preference (trigger: first calendar integration) - Team invite (trigger: user creates 5th task)

rule.statement: Pre-fill all inferable defaults (timezone, language, plan tier) and stop asking for information the product can detect or defer.
rule.rationale: Every required field that can be auto-detected or collected later reduces drop-off without data loss.

rule.statement: Show a progress checklist with 3-7 items and a completion percentage. Celebrate each completion. Never show an empty product state without a concrete single primary action.
rule.rationale: Progress indicators increase checklist completion rate by 15%. Empty states with no action ("create your first project" with no template) force users to think before they have context.
